items.notes: A true expression of the Marchesi de' Frescobaldi style, this wine shows impressive balance with strong aging potential, evolving beautifully over 8 to 12 years. Brunello leans into a more earthy, savoury profile than many New World wines, which is exactly what makes it so compelling. Expect layered notes of herbal spice and cherry, leading into a long, structured finish. One of my favourite wines from an excellent producer, offering great value for its quality.
items.pairings: Given its Italian origin, I prefer pairing this wine with classic regional dishes. It shines with the four Roman pastas, especially Pasta all’Amatriciana, where the herbal notes complement the savoury sausage, even more so with fennel. It’s equally enjoyable with a charcuterie board, particularly alongside Pecorino Romano, whose salty, sheep’s milk profile enhances the wine’s structure and balance.

items.notes: The first ever 100 point wine in Canada, and it more than lives up to the recognition. This Bordeaux blend from Mission Hill Family Estate has been crafted since 1997, typically built around Merlot with Cabernet Sauvignon, Cabernet Franc, and Petit Verdot adding depth and structure. The balance, structure, and finish are all exceptional. What stands out most is how refined and smooth the tannins are, paired with remarkable aging potential. The flavour depth is impressive, layered with dark fruit, subtle spice, and a touch of savoury complexity, yet never overly dry. One of the best glasses I’ve had in the Okanagan.
items.pairings: This wine pairs beautifully with rich, indulgent flavours that match its depth. Dark chocolate is a standout, highlighting the wine’s dark fruit and subtle spice, while dishes like grilled lamb or a well-seared steak bring out its structure and complexity. It also complements aged cheeses, adding another layer to the overall experience.

items.notes: One of my favourite bottles, this classic GSM blend from Châteauneuf-du-Pape shows exactly why the region is so respected. With Syrah bringing subtle pepper and Grenache adding a ripe, fruit-forward profile, it comes together in a beautifully balanced expression. The 2022 vintage is already incredibly smooth, with a refined dryness and great structure, yet still has the potential to evolve over the next 5 to 7 years as it reaches its full potential.
items.pairings:
Pairs best with rich, savoury dishes like grilled lamb, rosemary chicken, or braised short ribs, where the protein helps soften the tannins and bring out the structure. Also works really well with herb driven, Mediterranean style food and aged cheeses like Comté or Manchego.

That said, my ultimate pairing is actually Toblerone, don’t knock it till you try it.

items.notes:
A Bordeaux-style blend from Black Hills Estate Winery that highlights why blends can offer more complexity than single varietals. Built around Cabernet Sauvignon with Merlot and Cabernet Franc, it brings structure without feeling overly heavy, making it both approachable now and built to evolve. As it stands, the glass is already impressive, but the real potential lies in how the finish will continue to lengthen and smooth out over time.

What stood out most to me was the balance between spice and fruit, giving it a layered, expressive profile. It reminded me of the Oculus 2020 in terms of depth, but at this price point, I’m taking this every time.
items.pairings: Pairs really well with classic, hearty dishes that match its structure. It goes great with tenderloin or agnello, an Italian lamb dish, where the fat helps soften the tannins and bring out the fruit. It also works nicely with roasted vegetables and softer cheeses like Brie.
